sentences.text: Well, there wasn't any food, as I told you.
I was sick, I had TB.
My parents were trying to find some place where there would be food.
Just because we were out of the bunker, it didn't mean that there was food around.
People were eating horses that had been slaughtered in war, that was part of the, you know, food.
So I know there was a scarcity of food.
And I was severely undernourished.
And I not only had TB but because of this situation, my whole-- what would I call it-- my female internal organs were affected by this later, a doctor told me so.
There were repercussions to this.

So my parents were trying to get me into a sanitarium, and they were told that if one of them joined the Communist Party and one joined the Socialist Party, by luck of the draw, you know, that would help, either one party or another would help to get me into a sanitarium.
So my mother joined the Communist Party, my dad joined the Socialist Party.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in time, they got a chance for me to go to-- to a sanitarium.
My mother took me there and I kicked and I screamed at the door.
I remember yelling and screaming, don't leave me, don't leave me.
But she left.
And the first night, I slept with a little doll that was given me by a neighbor.
It was filled with sand.
It was a matchbox and the limbs were filled with sand.
And I squeezed so hard on that doll that when I woke up in the morning, the whole bed was full of sand and the kids were making fun of me.
But I was tremendously [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] frightened.
And it was it-- it felt like just one more abandonment.
After the first one in the boarding home where she-- where mother couldn't be with me, then the Yugoslavian place where she surely wasn't with me.
And now this.
It was just-- it seemed like it was the last straw.
But I calmed down and I remember that the children were divided up.
The Jewish kids went on Saturday to pray in one area of-- it was a convent that we were in.
So the Jewish kids went to one place to pray and the Christian kids went on another day to another place.
And there are some pleasant memories I have with it, some games that we played and stuff.
But I also remember that one of the little girls was given a ribbon that was put in her hair by one of the nuns.
And I guess I had the nerve to ask, could I have one please.
And she says, no, your parents are Jewish, they have enough money for that.
And I felt very crushed.
And I don't know in what sense I felt crushed.
Did I feel crushed like a little girl, you know, can't get the ribbon?
Or did I feel crushed feeling like, oh, here we go again?
I don't know.
But it made a big, big impact on me.
When I got back from the sanatorium, mother took me to these gatherings where they would sing Russian kind of workers unite kind of songs.
And I know I love that.
But she also took me to a film where scenes from the concentration camp were shown, and I was horrified.
And I don't know why she took me.
And she would say like, OK, don't look, don't look.
But it was there in front of me, and it was, it was horrible.
The net result of that was that I never wanted to find out about what happened in concentration camps.
I was afraid to read about it.
I didn't want to talk about it.
I mean as an adult, it was just [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- I don't know what to say.
It was just a terrible experience.
It was terrible for the people that it happened to.
It was terrible to show it to a child that young.
It was terrible to-- for me as a child to have realized that I could have been one of those people.
[SOBBING] And it was just an overwhelming experience, I guess.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When-- when I tried to get some kind of spiritual support or leadership from my parents, I didn't get it because my dad didn't have it in his background.
I think my mother out of her rebellion, there was just nothing.
And I had a severe need for something.
And I remember going out on a balcony and praying, looking up at the stars and praying.
I don't know if I stood or I knelt.
But I know I was looking for something.
And maybe it was to try to put all this together, [SOBBING] I don't know.
But because I didn't get it from my parents, I started quizzing our cleaning ladies.
And of course, they told me about Jesus, and I was very interested in hearing the stories.
And I was very adept and I'd go to school and I'd say to the Catholic kids, have you heard this story yet?
And I'd be like ahead of them, because my interest was just so intense and so propelling.
And I-- the only Jewish religious leadership I got at that time from my parents was because my dad was the manager of JOIN.
They-- they had a group Passover dinner.
And I was the youngest, and I was to ask the four questions.
And I hated Hebrew school.
I told you, I didn't know any Hebrew.
And so my mother phonetically typed out the four questions.

So I'm holding up this book and, with great feeling, asking the four questions, to the point where the rabbi said to my parents, you know, this child is very talented, you should do something, and so on.
And the next night I refused to go because it was sham.
And I did not want to participate in it.
And I was left home by myself.
They went and I was left home, but I felt very strongly about that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The experiences I had after the war, I think, maybe shook me up more than the experiences during the war.
I don't know why that is.
But I have a feeling it's so.
I'd walk across a playground and see the-- I'm laughing because you can't see me do this-- but the cut-off leg of some person.
And I'd go like-- you know, what is this.
And I came in contact, about as much contact as I was going to, with the meaning of man's inhumanity to man, by seeing it and smelling it.
So that made a big, big impression on me.
Even the fact that the horses were being slaughtered for food, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Didn't seem right to me.
But anyway, in school, I don't remember being discriminated against, as you say.
And when it was time for me to go to America, the kids all wrote in my autograph book.
And I have a lovely teacher.
I just loved her dearly.
And she wrote in my autograph book.
And it was just a very big deal, you know, and I was going to America.
Well, I arrived here in '47, June '47.
And my aunt, the one who went to Albania, made it out to the United States, and she sponsored us.
My aunt had a very weird thing.
When she realized that we were stuck in Hungary-- she loved music.
She refused to listen to music until we came out.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she did me some harm, but her will, you know, she was good willed.
We-- the story is is this that we could have come out to him in '38.
And I only know this vaguely.
But it's like the boat just left.
The last boat left, we would have been the next boat.
We were on the list, but it was too late.
So they made it, and so she sponsored us.
When I, when I came here, I was-- well, OK, no, I'm skipping a little bit.
When it was time to come to America, there was a dilemma of going to the American consulate.
And my mom was advised by one person to admit that she's been in the Communist Party in order for me to go to the sanitarium.
Because she never did anything, but we went to the singing stuff.
And somebody else, somebody worked for the consulate.
Somebody else advised her to admit it and somebody else said not to admit it.
My father told her to not admit it.
And she, being a very strong-headed woman, decided she would admit it.
And of course, they wouldn't let her come out to America.
So it was my father and I who could come out, and she had to stay behind.
When she stayed behind, she, because she wasn't really a communist, she had to fend for herself.
And what she did is she had a live-in-- a live-in-- moving kindergarten.
She picked up children all along the route between Buda and Pest, and then they went up to the mountains, rain or shine, and then she brought the kids back.
And so she indeed was not a communist.
Had she been one, she could have worked for the communist kindergarten system.
So in any case, she was left behind.
Everybody was very excited about us coming.
And father's friend, who was a writer, wrote me a poem that I have in my autograph book.
And when the time came to leave, it's very interesting.
And I only have this memory a couple of years ago.
We went by train from Hungary to Vienna, and mother standing on the platform and she's crying.
And before we left, I'm comforting her.
Soon, you'll be with us, you'll be with us.
And as the train is moving out of the platform, the thought comes to me, I'm leaving you now.
So I know that that abandonment was just a big issue.
I know it from later life, too.
But anyway, so I remember that.
But when we arrived in New York with my uncle, who made it from Czechoslovakia-- they went to Czechoslovakia.
They went there and within a few months they could come here.

But they had to leave their child in Czechoslovakia because they were afraid that the child would be a burden to the American sponsor or whatever-- was afraid that the child would be a burden and they couldn't work.
So that child was left in Czechoslovakia for two years and came out later.
Anyway, this uncle co-sponsored us in Madison, New York.
And then we came to Chicago.
And one of my aunt's first comments to me was, you've suffered enough as a Jew.
Don't tell them you are a Jew.
So I go, OK, because you know I'm already, I've already heard about Jesus.
I'm already interested in that, I've been a Catholic.
You know, I've never really been a Jew in terms of a practicing Jew.
So, OK.
So then the question was what religion to be.
And again, this old tricksterism that I learned, I guess.
I go to the girl that I played with.
She asks me what religion are you.
I go, what religion are you, I don't know how to say it in English.
She says Lutheran, I said, yeah, that's what I am, I'm Lutheran.
So I became a Lutheran, long enough until I was 13, when you no longer go to Sunday school and you no longer hear the stories, but you're supposed to go to church.
And in church, the-- the minister starts doing this hell stone and brimstone whatever.
And I go up to him very promptly after that message and I say, you can't tell me that millions of millions of Jews that don't believe in Jesus are going to go to hell, can you.
I mean, I didn't say you can't tell me.
You know, do you believe?
And he looked at me and said, my daughter, that is the tragedy of it.
And I walked out of that church and I never went back.
It's Bond Chapel.
It was a Missouri Synod, Bond Chapel at the University of Chicago.
And then I went looking for another Lutheran church, walked into another one, asked the minister the same question.
This was a Swedish Lutheran.
I asked him the same question and he said, you know, I don't know the answer to that, but God, in his infinite wisdom, you know, loves all people.
OK, fine.
So I went.
And again, I went through the confirmation.
And it was very interesting and I learned lots of things, only to start going to church.
And I was bored, I didn't get anything out of it.
I was tormented, I was literally tormented.
I didn't know what to do at this point.
And I went to my cousin, who was the son of this aunt who told me not to be Jewish.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just wanted to mention parenthetically, before I forget, when I got to America, [BACKGROUND NOISE] I had a real complex about Jewishness.
When I read, every page I looked at that had a J in it would just like jump out at me.
And I would have to check and see, are they saying something about the Jews?
And it was like a fixation, I guess.
So anyway, this cousin says-- I say to the cousin, I don't know what to do, here's what's happening.
And he says, well, why don't you go to the Sinai Temple, which is a very reformed temple in Hyde Park and see if they can help you.
And I said to him, but I am afraid.
I now know about Jesus and if I leave that knowledge behind, then what will happen.
And he said go talk to Dr.
Mann at Sinai Temple, and I did.
And Dr.
Mann looked at me and he said, is your mother Jewish.
Yes.
Is your father Jewish?
Yes.
What are you doing over there?
Come here.
So I went, and I also found it very interesting, until after confirmation, then I started going to temple.
And I start perceiving-- now that this is perception-- I start perceiving that the man is posturing on stage.
People are wearing their best furs.
You can't go to a service, a high service, unless you've paid a whole bunch of money, didn't seem right to me.
So I floundered around for a real long time, looking at different religions.
What was your family's reaction to this?
To my looking at other religions?
They didn't-- they were not bothered by it.
I was with my father, who was the continental Jew.
And my mother was in Europe, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she probably thought my grandfather would turn over in his grave.
But I forgot to tell you that after the war, because she couldn't have a Christmas branch, I got a Christmas tree, fully lit, all the presents and everything.
So my mother was not bound to the Jewish religion, as such.
Well, she was bound and she-- but she was rebelling, I guess, is the story.
So I did not get any flack.
My mother, when she came to America, she had Passover dinners and invited my children.
But didn't I think when the children came.
I don't think she did it before that.
Yeah, I had-- that-- that year that I had Christmas, I had Hanukkah and Christmas.
No, did not have Hanukkah, either.
Not until-- no, did not have Hanukkah, until my children came to visit my mother and then my roots became very important to me.
And they became very important to my mother.
And then some of these holidays were celebrated, but Passover was the main-- main holiday that was celebrated.
She came in 1956.
I was 18 and 1/2.
Yes.
I was sorry for her.
Here, I knew-- I knew the country.
She had too much makeup on.
She wasn't the way I remembered her.
But the thing was is that she now wanted me to be her little girl.
And at the same time, I needed to be the adult which I had to be from the time I got to America.
I had to write all the letters for my father.
I wrote the letters to get my mother out.
First, to get the Americans to let my mother in, and then my mother took care of the Russians, trying to get out.
But anyway, I was the main support system as the one who knew English.
And so now I was that for my mother.
At the same time, then I have to be her little girl.
So that took a little while, you know, to reconcile.
And then with her being here, I got some of the stories that I know now that I didn't know, some of the stories I'm telling you I didn't know about, but later in life, much later.
And what happened is, there was an animosity I had toward my mother that other people noticed that I didn't.
I spoke more roughly with her.
And people said, why are you mad at your mother.
And I said, I'm not mad at her.

But again, I figured out later that this is the resentment that was coming through of all the past years where-- I didn't know about the street car thing where there I thought she was great, I thought she was great giving away her food.
But as far as mothering me, I felt resentful that I was not taken care of.
I was put out there in the world, you know, to fend for myself.
Yes, they put me back in the second grade.
Yes.
High school and college.
Yes.
I have three children.
Yes, well, yes, the oldest one is Endre, and he's 27.
He's a psychologist.
And the middle one is Christy.
I named her for my best girlfriend.
And she's-- she was born in 1971, so we can figure out how old she is because I always forget.
And the youngest one-- no, she was born in 1971, yeah.
And the youngest boy was born November 18, his name is Lorant.
And he was born in 1970, he's a chemist.
And my daughter is now working at a bookstore, but she will go back to school to get her MA, so.
I'm a Montessori teacher.
I have a little school of my own in Mundelein.
No, in fact, I never associated myself with the holocaust because I didn't, quote, unquote, 'suffer.' and I saw the kind of suffering that had taken place.
And I was very afraid.
I did not want to read any books about the holocaust.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- really was afraid to find out what it really was, like what is it that I missed.
And I did not read any until my adult life.
And the first book I read was by Bettelheim, he was one of my professors at the U of C.
And I did read that book.
And from then, I slowly ventured into reading.
But I didn't associate myself as being a survivor until I started reading books about child survivors.
And among them, they included children who were in hiding.
And even though I wasn't in hiding with strangers, as my cousins were.
They lived in France and they were sent to Switzerland and they lived with strangers.
I wasn't hiding in that sense, but I was in hiding with the papers.
So I felt and I still feel reluctant to call myself a survivor, except that I did survive.
And I wouldn't have if I hadn't had the papers.
My grandparents who stayed in Vienna did not survive, they died on the cattle cars.
I have other relatives that did not survive because they were directly in the work camps or on their way-- I mean, to the concentration camps or on their way to the concentration camps.
However, I feel very strongly about being a Jew.
And if anybody says anything about a Jew, I will be the first one to say I am a Jew, even though I'm a convert.
I feel very important to say that I am a Jew.
When my children got older, I enrolled them in a Hungarian school so they could practice Hungarian.
And I have very strong feelings about Hungary.
They're good feelings.
And my husband is Hungarian, and so I wanted them to have that exposure, only to find out that the people there were quite Anti-Semitism.
And when the first comment was made about a school in Northbrook where this lady found toilet paper on the floor-- oh, the Jews.
I said, I am a Jew, how can you say that?
Anybody can throw toilet paper on the floor.
And the woman turned around and she said, oh, no, no, I'm part Jew also, you know, a little percent Jew.
But that's just an example.
My ears are open to any kind of accusation.
If my friends make any kind of comment, they won't now because they know how I feel.
When my children went to school, I identified myself as being Jewish-- convert, but being Jewish.
And when my friends now sometimes make comments about they're tired of hearing of, you know, Holocaust survivors telling their story, other people perished here in this, I say yes, they did.
But this, you know, I don't argue with them, as a matter of fact.
I just make the point, yes, others did, but the Jews were [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] selected in a very, very large number with the purpose of totally annihilating them.
And I try, I try to say what I can here.
But because they're my friends, it hurts that they should feel this way.
And as a matter of fact, I wouldn't have done this tape if it hadn't been [SOBBING] that I want my children to always remember where they came from.
And I never want any group to say that it never happened.
[CRYING] And I'm very frightened.

[CRYING] I'm very frightened of the future, to the point where I thought twice that you know in the last minute about making this tape because I thought, what if the wrong people get this, and I don't want anything to happen to my kids.
[SOBBING] My husband, who is not religious, had the children baptized, you know, wanted them to be baptized Catholic at the time.
I was not.
[SOBBING] And he only told me years later that he did it because of his own fear, that something could happen.
[SOBBING] And he lived in a little village where there was-- I'm sure there was Anti-Semitism because there was when I returned there with him.
But when the Jews were marched there, he says that the villagers gave them food.
So [CRYING] people are of two-- two minds and two hearts.
You know, on the one hand they give food.
And on the other hand, they disparage anybody that is not like them.
[CRYING]
